Note to Subscribers - Newsletter Delivery

I have published the daily digest of Best in Energy news and research every business day since 2014 and my own newsletter roughly three times a week since late 2024. The only exceptions are public holidays and about five weeks of annual leave each year. If am going on holiday or otherwise unable to publish for more than one day, I always notify subscribers in advance, usually via a caption at the top of the preceding daily digest. If you suddenly stop receiving emails from me without warning, the most likely reason is your subscription has expired or your email system has begun to refuse delivery.

Distributing newsletters via email involves playing a constantly evolving cat-and-mouse game with corporate email security systems designed to counter spam, phishing and other malicious activity. The systems are set up to block and refuse delivery to anything that looks like spam and can be triggered by individual words in the text or simply the fact multiple people in the same organisation receive the same email at the same time. Every time spam filters are upgraded, which happens several times each year, there is a risk emails that were previously delivered safely will start to be refused.

Like other newsletter publishers, I use bulk mailing software (in my case a system called MailerLite). If your email system refuses delivery more than about half a dozen times consecutively, the mailing software will automatically remove your address from the mailing list with no input from me and no warning. This is an anti-spam measure and a legal requirement in most countries designed to prevent abuse.

To ensure uninterrupted delivery of the daily digest and my newsletters, please consider adding my email address (the one used to send this email john@jkempenergy.com) to your contacts or safe senders list. If you suddenly stop receiving emails from me, check your spam filter, speak with your corporate email department, or reach out to me and I will investigate. The problem of non-delivery can almost always be fixed but only once we are aware of it.

Very best wishes
John

Copper inventories monitored by the three major futures exchanges fell by 54.7 kt last week to 1.088 million tons, extending the recent drawdown trend and reinforcing signs of tightening availability outside the United States. The decline was led by the SHFE, where stocks dropped 44.3 kt to 144 kt, the lowest level in six months, while LME-monitored inventories fell by a further 12 kt. In contrast, COMEX stocks rose by 1.6 kt, marking a tenth consecutive weekly increase as metal continues to be drawn into the US ahead of a potential copper tariff announcement expected by the end of June.

The divergence highlights the ongoing regional dislocation in the copper market. Traders and merchants have spent months redirecting material towards the US in anticipation of import restrictions, helping lift COMEX inventories to record levels while simultaneously draining stocks elsewhere. As a result, COMEX now accounts for 54% of all exchange-monitored copper inventories, a remarkable shift from historical norms, especially considering the nations relative small consumption level compared with the rest of the world.

Despite the recent increase in visible stocks, the broader market remains focused on a tightening medium-term outlook. The continued drawdown across Shanghai and London suggests underlying physical demand remains resilient, while concerns about mine supply growth, electrification demand, and the potential impact of US trade measures continue to support a constructive longer-term outlook for copper.

Over the past seven weeks, managed money's net long position in Brent crude has collapsed from a 7½-year high of 496k contracts to a 2026 low at 114k contracts in the reporting week ending 16 June, when prospects of a US-Iran peace deal gathered momentum and Brent subsequently tumbled 14%. The scale of the liquidation highlights how quickly speculative sentiment has swung from fears of a prolonged supply disruption to expectations of a sizeable supply surge once flows through the Strait of Hormuz normalise.

However, as highlighted previously, last week's 45% reduction in the net long was driven primarily by aggressive short selling rather than long liquidation. Gross short positions surged to 231k contracts, the highest level since the pandemic, while long exposure remained relatively elevated. This leaves the market increasingly vulnerable to a short-covering rally should the expected supply recovery prove slower than currently priced, or if traders are forced to reassess what given the present situation constitutes a fundamentally justified crude price.

Note weekly COT data from the CFTC will be published Monday due to Friday's Juneteenth holiday.

Sunday Market Update

Week of June 22 – June 26, 2026
Published: Sunday, June 21, 2026

Market Overview
Markets enter the final full trading week of June adjusting to structural shifts following the mid-month Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) interest rate decision. Macro desks are closely monitoring the fallout from the Fed's latest economic projections, which signaled a potential interest rate hike later in 2026. This hawkish bias pushed the U.S. dollar index to its highest level in over a year, creating immediate headwinds for risk assets and multinational equity lines.

Commodities are absorbing significant fundamental updates as the summer pricing cycle intensifies. The energy complex faced aggressive selling pressure as accelerating diplomatic progress regarding the unhindered flow of commercial vessels through the critical Strait of Hormuz shipping corridor directly unwound the global risk premium. In agriculture, row crops continue to battle macro pressure from the surging greenback alongside favorable Midwest weather models. 

Conversely, livestock desks are parsing a highly dynamic fundamental backdrop following an early release of the USDA Cattle on Feed report, setting up a competitive environment for cash positioning ahead of the massive USDA Acreage and Grain Stocks reports.

Last Week’s Review
E-mini S&P 500 (/ES): Pulled back from local technical shelves as the hawkish Fed policy tone sparked fresh macro caution.
Nasdaq-100 (/NQ): Faced localized rotational selling as higher-for-longer yield realities curbed near-term growth expansion.
Dow (/YM): Displayed moderate defensive stability but tracked the broader index consolidation into the weekend.
Russell 2000 (/RTY): Small caps bore the brunt of the dollar surge and restrictive borrowing cost outlook.

Hot Instruments: Last Week’s Movers
Stocks: Mixed and defensive sector rotations dominated trading following the hawkish central bank pause.

Commodities: WTI crude oil tumbled below key support on the de-escalation of shipping anxieties; grain and oilseed contracts remained pinned near structural lows.
Crypto: Bitcoin and Ethereum experienced minor technical leakage as the stronger dollar restricted baseline risk allocations.

This Week: Major News and Calendar Highlights
Mon, June 22: Existing Home Sales - Key barometer for housing sector health under restrictive interest rates
Tue, June 23: S&P Flash U.S. Manufacturing & Services PMI - Early look at mid-summer economic expansion metrics.

Wed, June 24: Crude Oil Inventories - Real-time metrics tracking domestic fuel draws and processing clips
Thu, June 25: USDA Quarterly Hogs and Pigs Report (3:00 PM CT) and Final GDP - Critical baseline metrics for livestock farrowing intentions and macro economic health.

Fri, June 26: Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index - The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge to close out the month

Equity Index Futures
S&P 500 (/ES) Sep ’26: Last Price ~7,431.46, Trend Neutral, Support 7,350 / 7,280

Nasdaq-100 (/NQ) Sep ’26: Last Price ~28,740, Trend Neutral/Bearish, Support 28,100

Dow Jones (/YM) Sep ’26: Last Price ~48,950, Trend Neutral, Support 48,200

S&P 500: Consolidating inside a tightening technical range; watch for initial volume support to be tested on early weekly gaps.

Nasdaq-100: Vulnerable to sudden capital outflows if long-term Treasury yields continue to press local technical limits.

Dow: Benefiting slightly from blue-chip defensive allocations away from interest-rate-sensitive growth desks.

Bonds & Interest Rate Futures
10-Year Note (/ZN): Level ~113’04, Support 112’12, Resistance 114’16, Outlook Bearish

30-Year Bond (/ZB): Level ~110’24, Support 109’16, Resistance 112’04, Outlook Bearish

2-Year Note (/ZT): Level ~102’08, Support 101’16, Resistance 103’04, Outlook Fed-sensitive

Yield Curve: Experienced notable flattening pressure as short-term debt instruments sold off to reflect the threat of a late-2026 interest rate hike.

Key Level: Yields holding firm near summer highs, presenting a significant headwind for standard margin allocations across commercial commodity desks.

Grains Markets
Corn (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$4.12 3/4, Support $4.05, Resistance $4.22, Outlook Bearish

Soybeans (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$11.13 1/2, Support $10.95, Resistance $11.35, Outlook Bearish

Soybean Oil (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$69.67, Support $67.50, Resistance $71.80, Outlook Bearish

Wheat SRW (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$5.84 1/2, Support $5.68, Resistance $5.98, Outlook Neutral/Bearish

South America: Deep seasonal harvest flows from Brazil and Argentina continue to flood global export channels, keeping domestic cash prices defensive.

Global Demand: Commercial grain desks remain entirely uninspired as the multi-year peak in the U.S. dollar severely limits structural export competitiveness.

Liquidity: Row crops remain technically damaged with routine short-covering bounces capped by heavy commercial selling, as wet and cool Midwest weather patterns provide an excellent start for early crop development.

Livestock Markets
Live Cattle (Aug ’26): Last Price ~$241.17 1/2, Support $239.00, Resistance $244.50, Outlook Constructive

Feeder Cattle (Aug ’26): Last Price ~$357.42 1/2, Support $353.00, Resistance $362.50, Outlook Positive

Cattle: Front-month contracts found solid technical backing into the weekend after the June 1 Cattle on Feed report printed a 10% year-over-year decline in May placements, matching the low end of trade expectations.

Cash Trade: The total inventory on feed came in at 11.7 million head, 2% above last year, which analysts view as neutral due to lower marketings. Direct cash trades remained firm at $255 to $258, while wholesale choice boxed beef values stayed consolidated at $392.70 amidst Ongoing New World screwworm monitoring.

Energy Markets
WTI Crude (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$84.88, Support $81.50, Resistance $86.50, Outlook Bearish

Natural Gas (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$3.12, Support $2.90, Resistance $3.30, Outlook Constructive

Crude: Prices suffered an aggressive fundamental wash, testing multi-month lows near $84.88 as the stabilization of the Strait of Hormuz transit lanes successfully drained the geopolitical risk premium.

Fuel Costs: Refiners are balancing strong utilization metrics against shifting domestic freight data, with cash spreads remaining highly sensitive to near-term storage draws.

Metals Market
Gold (Aug ’26): Last Price ~$4,223.50, Support $4,150, Resistance $4,260, Outlook Bearish

Silver (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$72.10, Support $69.80, Resistance $73.50, Outlook Bearish

Platinum (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$1,845.00, Support $1,790, Resistance $1,890, Outlook Cautious

Copper (Jul ’26): Last Price ~$5.85, Support $5.65, Resistance $6.02, Outlook Cautious

Precious metals: Faced broad institutional liquidation as the combo of hot producer inflation data and a hawkish Fed hold completely broke intermediate support tiers.
Sentiment: Industrial and precious complexes alike are feeling the weight of the massive dollar index rally, leaving technical desks waiting for an active floor to establish.

Crypto Markets
Bitcoin (BTC): Price ~$63,262, Weekly Change Mixed, Outlook Neutral/Range

Ethereum (ETH): Price ~$3,480, Weekly Change Flat, Outlook Consolidation

Theme: Bitcoin continues to chop inside a strict trading band above the $60,000 level, attempting to find institutional interest amid broader macro asset consolidation.

Trading Takeaways
Respect Volatility: Keep execution sizes light as the market works through the crucial end-of-quarter positional adjustments and Friday's PCE inflation reading.

Relative Strength: Monitor livestock contracts to see if the tight May placement metrics allow cattle to detach further from the broader commodity sell-off.

Risk Management: Establish firm stops ahead of Thursday's Hogs and Pigs report to isolate accounts from sudden mid-session gaps.

Top Watches for the Week
PCE Price Index (Fri): The critical macroeconomic data release for validating whether the Fed's hawkish rate stance will persist into autumn.

Crude Oil $81.50 Support: Watching to see if industrial buyers step back into the energy complex once the geopolitical unwinding concludes.

Midwest Moisture Conditions: Tracking real-time heat maps across the Western Corn Belt ahead of next week's highly anticipated USDA Acreage report.

Summary
This trading week marks a transition into late-summer price Discovery. With a hawkish Federal Reserve backdrop, high-stakes inflation metrics, and pivotal livestock reports hitting the tape, maintaining an active, risk-averse execution structure is vital.

Futures Joke of the Week
A futures trader told his friend he finally built a completely objective, non-emotional trading system...

Then the Fed hinted at another rate hike and his crude position broke support, and now he is out in the machinery lot trying to see if his old grain truck can be legally licensed to haul gravel for county road maintenance! 🤣🤣

🚨 Trump’s new warning has put Lebanon at the centre of the US-Iran talks in Switzerland.

As negotiations continue at Bürgenstock, Trump warned Iran to stop its “proxies” in Lebanon from escalating violence, saying the US could strike Iran again if attacks continue.

🛡️ @JDVance says the Switzerland process is now a technical negotiation, not a final settlement. The main agenda remains Iran’s nuclear programme, Lebanon ceasefire enforcement, frozen Iranian assets, oil-export licences, sanctions relief and Strait of Hormuz stability.

Vance has claimed progress on Hormuz and Iran’s nuclear weapons path, but the harder issues are still unresolved: verification, uranium enrichment, IAEA oversight, sanctions timelines and whether fighting in Lebanon can actually stop.

Iran’s position is that the MoU requires ceasefire implementation on all fronts, including Lebanon. Israel says it is not bound by the US-Iran deal and will keep acting against threats.

#iran #usa #trump #vance #lebanon #israel #hormuz #oil #geopolitics #diplomacy

quotedTweet.text:
🚨 Vance says US-Iran talks have made “great progress”, but the hardest part is still ahead.

After talks in Switzerland, US Vice President JD Vance said Washington has already achieved two key objectives: reopening the Strait of Hormuz and ending Iran’s nuclear weapons path.

But this should be read as the US position — not a completed final agreement.

The Strait of Hormuz is still sensitive. The US says commercial traffic is continuing, while Iran-linked warnings after Israeli strikes in Lebanon have kept shipping risk elevated.

🛡️ On the nuclear side, Iran says it can give written assurances that it has no intention of building a bomb, but Tehran is still refusing to abandon uranium enrichment.

That means the real negotiation now is about verification, enriched uranium stockpiles, IAEA oversight, sanctions relief, frozen assets, oil-export licences and Lebanon ceasefire enforcement.

#iran #usa #hormuz #oil #lebanon #nuclear #qatar #pakistan #geopolitics #diplomacy

text:
🇺🇸 Trump says :

The United States could become the "guardian" of the Strait of Hormuz and potentially collect transit tolls to secure one of the world's most strategic maritime chokepoints.

The Strait handles a significant share of global oil exports, making any discussion about its security, control, or taxation a matter of global economic and geopolitical importance.

If such a policy were ever pursued, it would reshape energy markets, maritime security, and power dynamics across the Middle East.

Trump tells Fox News the US could assume control of the Strait of Hormuz, charge transit fees, and claim a share of oil flows. He described America as the security guarantor of the waterway and the broader Middle East. https://t.co/opy34TScCF
@walkinintheyard I heard that too, but crude inventory numbers don’t seem to back that up? Not sure why.
Second, Gipitee’s plot of crude inventories (not including SPR), it’s looking pretty normal. https://t.co/TlSVHafOKg

The toll question reveals the underlying leverage structure. If the US imposes one, it claims formal control over an international waterway — legally complex, especially with China, India, and Japan watching. If Iran imposes one unilaterally, it's a sanctions violation and a casus belli. Neither happens: the toll was always rhetorical positioning. What matters commercially is the insurance premium, which is already a de facto toll that markets pay regardless of official announcements. Brent at $80 with US crude inventories at 1985 lows is not a fearful price; it's a market that hasn't fully priced the physical picture yet.The toll question reveals the underlying leverage structure. @mikemcglone11 The key issue is that you’re extrapolating a regional balance  into a global crude price call.
The physical market still looks tight: US crude inventories remain below 5-year norms, Cushing sits roughly 37% below its 5-year average, and the Brent curve remains backwardated.

"The good news: The Strait of Hormuz is open again, after Iran and the United States signed a memorandum of understanding this week.

The bad news: It may be too late.

Oil hasn’t been coming out of the Middle East for nearly four months. All told, the world lost 1.15 billion barrels of oil supply during the war, according to analytics firm Kpler.
That has left the oil market in a precarious state, and it’s rapidly approaching a breaking point. 

The International Energy Administration’s strategic petroleum reserves are at their lowest levels since 1990. The American emergency reserve is at a 43-year low. And commercial inventories have hit operational stress levels.

“You want to see bedlam?” President Donald Trump said at the G7 in Versailles Wednesday. “We run out of reserves in about four weeks.”

Trump is right. But reopening the strait this week may not get oil out of the Persian Gulf fast enough to prevent crude inventories from effectively running to empty.

Oil prices may have to go higher again."

CNN

📊 Economic Data Preview: A Lighter Week Ahead (June 22–26, 2026)

As markets enter a notably quieter stretch of the economic calendar, investors have a clearer window to digest fresh readings on growth, inflation, labor conditions, and consumer behavior.

Monday, June 22 & Tuesday, June 23 No major U.S. economic releases are scheduled. This breathing room allows focus to shift toward positioning ahead of the data-heavy midweek.

Wednesday, June 24
Current Account Balance
EIA Crude Oil Inventories
MBA Mortgage Applications Index
New Home Sales
These figures will offer timely insights into external balances, energy supply dynamics, housing affordability and demand, and mortgage market activity, all sensitive to interest rate expectations.

Thursday, June 25 (Highlight Day)
Initial & Continuing Jobless Claims
Durable Goods Orders
EIA Natural Gas Inventories
Q2 GDP – Third Estimate
PCE Price Index (headline & core)
Personal Income & Spending
Thursday stands out as the most consequential session. The final GDP revision will refine our picture of economic expansion, while the PCE readings, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge, are likely to draw the sharpest attention for clues on price pressures and future policy direction. Labor market data and spending figures will round out the assessment of underlying momentum.

The week closes with forward-looking consumer confidence data alongside supply-chain and inventory metrics. Sentiment readings often preview shifts in household spending, which remains the backbone of U.S. economic activity.

With key growth, inflation, and confidence indicators on the docket, this data cluster could help shape near-term expectations for monetary policy and market sentiment heading into the summer.

Week-Ahead Brief | June 22-26 2026 | SPX 7500.58 close (Thursday OPEX)

The cheapest options of the year going into the biggest macro week. Five days mapped. Daily levels and scenarios.

Goldman warned Friday that this week looks calm but isn't. Here is why.

Three signals are all pricing the same thing. Monday options trade at 8.91 percent vol, the cheapest pricing since Christmas holiday week. COR1M sits at 8.02. Below 8 is extreme. When correlation snaps back, the move is usually violent. June OPEX cleared Thursday with more call positioning than puts, leaving dealer hedges to unwind Monday and Tuesday. Three asymmetric signals stacked into a week with Core PCE Thursday.

The week builds straight into PCE. Monday Fed Waller speech 9 AM kicks off post-FOMC commentary. Tuesday PMI Flash gives the first economic activity read. Wednesday brings Stress Test results plus Micron earnings after-hours. Thursday Core PCE is the headline event. Friday closes Q2 with pension rebalancing flow last 30 minutes.

Iran closed Hormuz again Saturday as talks open in Switzerland Sunday with Lebanon attacks still live. Watch Brent at the Monday open for the inflation read.

Thursday closed at 7500.58 and took back the pivot. 7500 anchors the week. 7440 to 7480 EMA confluence holds the floor. Above 7600 the post-OPEX residual call wall caps the upside. Below 7400 is the structural risk zone if PCE comes in hot.

THE VOL PICTURE

Vol is priced for a quiet week into PCE Thursday. That pricing is the asymmetric part of the setup.

Monday at-the-money options trade at 8.91 percent IV. Goldman flagged that level approaches the 7 percent lower bound seen during the Christmas holiday week. That is holiday-week pricing for a week that has Fed Waller speaking Monday morning, PMI Flash Tuesday, Stress Test results Wednesday, Core PCE Thursday, and quarter-end Friday. The options market is paying almost nothing for protection on Monday and Tuesday.

The straddles tell the same story. Monday options price an 85 point expected move from the close. Friday options price 110 points. The 25 point gap is the entire week of risk premium. Most of it lives in Thursday's PCE print and Friday's month-end flow.

Goldman also flagged the structural imbalance from Thursday's OPEX. The June expiry cycle had more call delta notional than puts. When those positions roll off, dealer hedges associated with them unwind, and that can contribute to elevated volatility. The institutional research desks are openly warning about Monday and Tuesday being trap days.

VVIX sits at 88.43, right at its historical mean of 85. After running to 94.53 Wednesday on the hawkish dot plot, vol-of-vol normalized into Thursday's OPEX rally. Going into a PCE week with the dot plot now showing rate hikes as a real possibility, VVIX at the historical baseline is not paying up for protection. That mirrors what the index options market is doing.

The vol curve says the market thinks Monday and Tuesday are sleepy then vol builds into PCE Thursday and quarter-end Friday. If post-OPEX dealer unwind hits as Goldman called out, that assumption breaks fast and Monday or Tuesday becomes the surprise vol expansion.

WHAT HAPPENED THURSDAY OPEX

SPX closed 7500.58, up 80.48 points. Opened 7487.36. Session low 7468.32. Session high 7511.07. Reclaimed the 7500 pivot that broke Wednesday post-FOMC. Closed RIGHT AT the level. Range only 42.75 points which is tight for an OPEX day. Vol absorbed cleanly.

Dow +0.14 percent. Russell 2000 +2.12 percent (smallcaps led). Nasdaq +1.91 percent on Intel-Apple chip news. Brent oil dropped on Iran deal optimism then bounced back to $80 Friday on Hormuz uncertainty.

AAII Sentiment for week ending 6/17 showed Bullish 36.6 percent, Bearish 39.4 percent. More bearish than bullish, more bearish than the historical average of 31 percent. That is a contrarian-bullish positioning setup, especially with smallcaps showing the strongest sentiment in the special question at 27.4 percent.

The Sep 15 Fed meeting now prices 50 percent No Change and 38 percent 25 bps Hike. Before Wednesday the hike probability was near zero. The hawkish dot plot repriced the curve. PCE Thursday is the next test.

THE WEEK'S BIG STORY

Four threads pull on this week.

1. Post-OPEX dealer unwind Monday and Tuesday. Goldman flagged June OPEX had more call delta notional than puts. When call hedges unwind without put hedges balancing them, the unwind tilts the tape. Vol can expand from compressed levels fast.

2. PCE Thursday is the gravity center. Core PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. After a hawkish dot plot now showing 38 percent hike probability at September, any upside surprise puts hike pricing above 50 percent. Below 7400 is the structural risk zone if PCE comes in hot.

3. Iran 60-day clock and Hormuz. Iran declared Hormuz closed Saturday, calling it the IRGC's first operational step. US-Iran talks open Sunday in Switzerland. Markets have so far dismissed the closure as rhetoric since 55 ships transited Hormuz Saturday despite the announcement. But Brent at $80 and Israel still attacking Lebanon mean energy can spike Monday open if Sunday talks fail. The peace deal is fragile.

4. Quarter-end Friday. Pension rebalancing flow possible last 30 minutes of the session. Q2 ends Friday June 26 since June 30 is a Tuesday. Watch closing tape.

THE WEEK'S RISKS

1. Goldman's post-OPEX dealer unwind warning Monday and Tuesday. Vol priced for calm. The map shows 7660 and 7650 call walls from OPEX residual on Monday that vanish Tuesday. The transition between days is where vol can fire.

2. PCE Thursday is the gravity center. Hawkish dot plot showed 38 percent hike probability at September meeting. Hot PCE puts hike pricing above 50 percent. Cool PCE relieves the entire curve.

3. Iran Hormuz closure and Lebanon. Talks open Switzerland Sunday. If they fail or stall, Brent pops Monday open and feeds the inflation story into PCE Thursday. 55 ships still transited Hormuz Saturday so the immediate impact is rhetoric not reality. But the situation is fragile.

4. Quarter-end Friday pension rebalancing. If Q2 ends positive, expect equity selling and bond buying last 30 minutes. Could be a setup for a Monday June 29 reversal if positioning unwinds.

RULES

1. Vol is priced at holiday-week levels. The setup is asymmetric. Trade smaller until the post-OPEX unwind window clears.

2. 7500 is the anchor for the entire week. Hold it Monday and bulls have a path. Lose it and the structure changes.

3. 7440 to 7480 is the moving average confluence zone. 20D EMA 7441.01 + 20D SMA 7481.88. The strongest technical support cluster of the week sits here.

4. Goldman warned about post-OPEX dealer unwind hitting Monday and Tuesday. Vol can expand fast from compressed levels. Treat all levels as breakable Monday and Tuesday.

5. The 7345 acceleration zone on Monday's put map is the worst-case break. Massive sellers stacked. If 7350 fails, the move accelerates.

6. PCE Thursday is the headline event. Position smaller into the print. The 8:30 AM data drop includes PCE + Durable Goods + GDP + Jobless Claims all at once. Most catalyst-heavy moment of the year so far.

7. The 7320 and 7310 paired levels on Thursday's book look like an insurance trade. Watch how they develop into PCE.

8. Friday is quarter-end. Last 30 minutes can be one-directional based on Q2 performance. Stay flat or hedge into the close.

9. Iran headlines from Sunday's Switzerland talks set the Monday open tone. If Brent pops, the inflation story for PCE Thursday gets louder.

10. Above 7500 we stay bullish into PCE. Below 7400 we stay bearish into PCE. The middle is the chop zone where most of the week lives.

Not financial advice. Verify all levels before trading.

One of the most world changing inventions is metal box containers.

In 1956, an American trucking entrepreneur named Malcolm McLean loaded 58 metal containers onto a converted oil tanker.

The idea sounded almost embarrassingly simple. https://t.co/mf7fYzUgRH

This connects with my earlier point:

Oil is no longer behaving like a pure inflation driver.

OPEC still exists, but its price-control power is weakening.
Demand is slowing, supply is recovering, and some producers are choosing expansion over coordination.

The market is not only pricing inflation.
It is pricing the Fed’s fear of being wrong.

Observing trust structures, not price noise.
One Structure, Many Signals.

A Lawyer Looks at the MoU
by Hugh Fitzgerald

Daniel Pomerantz is an Israeli lawyer who takes a look at the MoU and breaks it down for us section by section. He raises some issues — such as the economic effect of the agreement on China — that I have not seen discussed anywhere else.

"Global oil markets will be flooded as never before — not only will the Strait of Hormuz open, along with its backlog of tankers, but the UAE has left OPEC, Venezuela and America have increased oil production, and even Iranian oil will join global markets."

Venezuela, with the world’s largest oil reserves, after the U.S. seizure of Nicolás Maduro, has had all American sanctions on its oil sales lifted, and American companies are now helping the Venezuelans to repair those wells that had fallen into desuetude. Oil production is at an eight-year high, and continues to rise.

The UAE has quit OPEC, because it no longer wanted to have Saudi Arabia, the linchpin of OPEC, limiting its output. The UAE is now producing about 3.5 mpd, but expects by the beginning of 2027 to produce more than 5 mpd.

The experience of having the Strait of Hormuz closed will no doubt lead the deep-pocketed Arab oil states to immediately start to build oil pipelines from the Gulf, across Saudi Arabia, most likely to the Red Sea, where tankers can take the oil either out onto the Arabian Sea to Asian markets, or up through the Red Sea to the Suez Canal and thence to European markets. A second route for a pipeline would run from Saudi Arabia through Jordan to Israel, where tankers at Haifa could then take the oil to European markets. Once these pipelines are ready, the Gulf oil will bypass the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran will lose its only economic weapon.

Iranian ballistic missiles and drones have hit all eight members of the Gulf Cooperation Council, with considerable damage to airports, oil facilities, hotels, and other clearly civilian targets. Determined not to be attacked in that way again, all of these states will be spending heavily on defense. Some of those countries will want to buy defensive weapons from Israel, as the UAE has already done. Iran’s attacks in the region could end up enriching Israel’s defense industry, a nice case of unintended consequences.

Read more at the link in the next post.

IBB said the same thing about SAP. He couldn’t resolve the problem he created even with 8 years in power and ultimate authority as a military head of state.

Subsidy and FX fixes are only one side of the problem. The simplest part to implement, IMO.

The real work is in fixing insecurity, diversifying our income, upgrading our power transmission lines, modernizing local agriculture, and ramping up oil production to meet our OPEC quota.

Until then, he’s just another president like GEJ to me.
